perturbation.original_text: The Compensation Options will be exercisable for a period of 24 months at the Offering Price.
perturbation.changed_text: The Compensation Options will be exercisable for a period of 6 months at the Offering Price.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the option exercise period to 6 months may violate securities regulations regarding fair and reasonable terms for compensation options, especially if it's considered an unreasonably short period that benefits the agents at the expense of shareholders.
perturbation.law_explanation: NI 45-106 outlines requirements for prospectus exemptions. While it doesn't specify an exact minimum term for compensation options, reducing the exercise period to 6 months could be deemed unreasonable, calling into question whether the offering meets the overall requirements for fairness and investor protection under securities laws which is generally required for prospectus exemptions. The securities commission could view a short exercise period as detrimental to potential investors.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Nebraska Legislature NEBRASKA LEGISLATURE The official site of the Nebraska Unicameral Legislature Nebraska Revised Statute 45-106 Revised Statutes Chapter 45 45-106 Print Friendly Chapter 45 45-106. Interest; warrants or orders. All warrants or orders issued by the proper authorities of any county, city, township, school district, other municipal subdivision less than a county, or sanitary district, whose area is located partly within a municipality, shall draw interest from and after the date of presentation for payment at such rate as is fixed by the issuing authority and endorsed on the warrant. All warrants issued by the state shall draw interest at the rate of four percent per annum from the date the same are presented for payment. Source Laws 1879, § 10, p. 115; Laws 1895, c. 43, § 1, p. 185; Laws 1895, c. 44, § 1, p. 186; Laws 1899, c. 48, § 1, p. 260; Laws 1903, c. 54, § 1, p. 345; R.S.1913, § 3355; Laws 1919, c. 108, § 1, p. 264; C.S.1922, § 2843; Laws 1929, c. 125, § 1, p. 472; C.S.1929, § 45-110; R.S.1943, § 45-106; Laws 1945, c. 110, § 1, p. 354; Laws 1947, c. 171, § 2, p. 519; Laws 1969, c. 51, § 109, p. 340. Cross References For other provisions as to interest on warrants: Counties, see section 23-1602 . Drainage districts, see section 31-416 . Frequent Questions Americans with Disabilities Act Notice Privacy Policy Glossary of Terms Contact Us Disclaimer OFFICIAL NEBRASKA GOVERNMENT WEBSITE Copyright © Nebraska Legislature, all rights reserved.
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the option exercise period from 24 months to 6 months. The Perturbation Explanation states this may violate securities regulations regarding fair and reasonable terms for compensation options, particularly if 6 months is considered unreasonably short and benefits agents at the expense of shareholders. The Law Explanation supports this, stating that while NI 45-106 doesn't specify an exact minimum term, a 6-month exercise period could be deemed unreasonable and call into question whether the offering meets the requirements for fairness and investor protection under securities laws. The modification contradicts the general principle of fairness required in securities laws, potentially disadvantaging investors, as highlighted in the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: Each notice shall be personally delivered to the addressee or sent by fax or e-mail to the addressee. A notice which is personally delivered or delivered by fax or e-mail shall, if delivered prior to 5:00 p.m. (Toronto time) on a Business Day, be deemed to be given and received on that day and, in any other case, be deemed to be given and received on the first Business Day following the day on which it is delivered.
perturbation.changed_text: Each notice shall be personally delivered to the addressee or sent by fax or e-mail to the addressee. A notice which is personally delivered or delivered by fax or e-mail shall be deemed to be given and received immediately upon sending, regardless of the time or day.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text states that a notice is deemed received immediately, regardless of the time it was sent. This could conflict with standard legal interpretations of 'reasonable notice' and accepted business practices, especially concerning critical legal deadlines. It may not allow reasonable time for the recipient to act on the notice, potentially voiding it.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Electronic Commerce Act, 2000 (Ontario) addresses when electronic documents are considered to be sent and received. While it allows for agreements to specify otherwise, the default rules in the Act aim to ensure reasonable certainty. The modified text's 'immediate receipt' clause may contradict the spirit of the Act and introduce ambiguities if it prevents a party from demonstrably accessing the information, especially given the potential for emails to be filtered or missed.
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a legal contradiction by stating that a notice is deemed received immediately upon sending, regardless of the time or day. This directly contradicts standard legal interpretations of 'reasonable notice', potentially violating the spirit of ensuring parties have adequate time to respond. The Law Explanation further highlights the Electronic Commerce Act, 2000 (Ontario), and its aim for 'reasonable certainty' when electronic documents are received. The changed text eliminates any consideration for reasonable access and processing time, leading to potential ambiguities and non-compliance.

perturbation.original_text: The Corporation covenants and agrees to protect, indemnify, and save harmless, each of the Agents and their respective affiliates, and each and every one of the directors, officers, employees, partners and agents of the Agents (individually, an "Indemnified Party" and collectively, the "Indemnified Parties") harmless from and against any and all expenses, losses (excluding loss of profits), claims, actions, damages (other than consequential or punitive damages) or liabilities, joint or several (including the aggregate amount paid in settlement of any actions, suits, proceedings or claims and the reasonable fees and expenses of their counsel that may be incurred in advising with respect to and/or defending any claim that may be made against the Indemnified Parties) to which any Indemnified Party may become subject or otherwise involved in any capacity under any statute or common law or otherwise insofar as such expenses, losses, claims, damages, liabilities or actions arise out of or are based, directly or indirectly, upon the performance of professional services rendered to the Corporation by the Indemnified Parties (or any of them), whether directly or indirectly, including by reason of:
perturbation.changed_text: The Corporation covenants and agrees to protect, indemnify, and save harmless, each of the Agents and their respective affiliates, and each and every one of the directors, officers, employees, partners and agents of the Agents (individually, an "Indemnified Party" and collectively, the "Indemnified Parties") harmless from and against any and all expenses, losses (including loss of profits), claims, actions, damages (including consequential or punitive damages) or liabilities, joint or several (including the aggregate amount paid in settlement of any actions, suits, proceedings or claims and the reasonable fees and expenses of their counsel that may be incurred in advising with respect to and/or defending any claim that may be made against the Indemnified Parties) to which any Indemnified Party may become subject or otherwise involved in any capacity under any statute or common law or otherwise insofar as such expenses, losses, claims, damages, liabilities or actions arise out of or are based, directly or indirectly, upon the performance of professional services rendered to the Corporation by the Indemnified Parties (or any of them), whether directly or indirectly, including by reason of:
perturbation.explanation: Removing the exclusion of consequential and punitive damages broadens the indemnity, potentially violating public policy and legal restrictions on indemnification for certain types of misconduct. Indemnifying against punitive damages, which are designed to punish the wrongdoer, could be deemed unenforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Ontario Securities Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.5, s. 137
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 137 of the Ontario Securities Act imposes liability for misrepresentations in prospectuses and other documents. While it doesn't explicitly prohibit indemnification, courts may refuse to enforce indemnity agreements that undermine the Act's purpose of protecting investors. Broadening the indemnity to include punitive damages arising from, for instance, a misrepresentation claim, could be seen as an attempt to shield the agents from the consequences of their own misconduct, thus contravening the Act's intent.

perturbation.justification: The modified text broadens the indemnity to include consequential and punitive damages, while the original text specifically excluded these. The Law Explanation indicates that such a broad indemnity could undermine the Ontario Securities Act's purpose of protecting investors by shielding agents from the consequences of their misconduct. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing sections on civil liability and misrepresentation (e.g., Section 130, 137), suggests the Act aims to hold parties accountable for misrepresentations. Indemnifying against punitive damages, which are intended to punish misconduct, directly contradicts this principle of accountability by potentially allowing agents to avoid the financial consequences of their actions. This creates a direct conflict with the policy objectives outlined in the Act as explained by the generated Law Explanation.
